evals.prompt: I have a Python function that searches our Postgres database. How do I make it available as a tool for a DSPy ReAct agent?
evals.expected_output: A solution showing the search function with type hints on all parameters and a clear docstring, passed to dspy.ReAct (implicitly or wrapped with dspy.Tool). Should note that the function should return a string, include dspy.configure(lm=...) with a generic provider, and show the agent calling pattern.

assertions.name: type_hints_on_tool
assertions.description: Emphasizes that all tool function parameters need type hints — DSPy infers the JSON schema from them; without hints the agent gets no parameter info

assertions.name: string_return_value
assertions.description: Notes that tools must return strings (not dicts, objects, or ORM results) — the agent sees the raw return value as an Observation

assertions.name: react_integration
assertions.description: Shows passing the tool (directly or as dspy.Tool) to dspy.ReAct tools list

assertions.name: provider_agnostic_lm
assertions.description: LM configuration uses a generic provider string with an alternative comment, not a hardcoded single provider

evals.prompt: I need my DSPy agent to run arbitrary Python code safely in a sandbox. How do I set up PythonInterpreter?
evals.expected_output: Shows dspy.PythonInterpreter used as a context manager with the 'with' statement, mentions that Deno must be installed as a prerequisite, demonstrates at least one selective permission parameter (enable_read_paths, enable_network_access, etc.), and shows or mentions integration with dspy.CodeAct.

assertions.name: context_manager_usage
assertions.description: Uses PythonInterpreter as a context manager with 'with PythonInterpreter() as interp:' — required to properly start and shut down the Deno/Pyodide process

assertions.name: deno_prerequisite_mentioned
assertions.description: Notes that Deno must be installed — the most common failure mode for new users

assertions.name: selective_permissions_shown
assertions.description: Shows at least one permission parameter (enable_read_paths, enable_write_paths, enable_network_access, or enable_env_vars) to demonstrate that permissions are off by default

assertions.name: codeact_integration
assertions.description: Mentions or shows dspy.CodeAct as the agent that uses PythonInterpreter for sandboxed code generation

evals.prompt: I have an MCP server running and want to use its tools in a DSPy agent. How do I convert MCP tools to DSPy tools?
evals.expected_output: Shows dspy.Tool.from_mcp_tool with a ClientSession setup using the mcp library, notes that converted tools are async so the agent must use aforward() not agent(), includes the pip install dspy[mcp] install step, and shows the full async context manager pattern.

assertions.name: from_mcp_tool_class_method
assertions.description: Uses dspy.Tool.from_mcp_tool — the specific class method for MCP conversion, not a custom wrapper

assertions.name: client_session_management
assertions.description: Shows ClientSession setup using the mcp library — DSPy does not manage the server connection, the user must

assertions.name: aforward_for_async_tools
assertions.description: Notes that MCP tools are async so agent.aforward() must be used instead of agent() — the most common gotcha

assertions.name: mcp_extra_install
assertions.description: Includes pip install 'dspy[mcp]' or equivalent — the MCP integration is an optional extra that must be installed separately
